name: Promontory Summit, UT
description: Golden Spike ceremony site May 10, 1869. First Transcontinental Railroad completion. National Historic Site with original railroad grade accessible. Both the Union Pacific and Central Pacific locomotives met here, surrounded by thousands of workers, dignitaries, and reporters. The immediate area was abandoned shortly after the mainline was rerouted through Ogden, leaving behind a dense scatter of artifacts from one of the most significant events in American railroad history. The abandoned grade and original tie positions are visible and yield railroad spikes, coins, and personal effects.

name: Corinne, UT
description: Corinne, Utah was a major railroad town and freight hub near the transcontinental line, once nicknamed the 'Burg on the Bear.' Established as a non-Mormon commercial center in 1869, it boomed as a supply depot for Montana mining camps, with constant wagon and rail traffic. By the 1880s the town largely emptied as rail routes shifted, making the townsite a productive metal detecting location with late 1860s-1880s coins, hardware, and commerce-related artifacts. Much of the original townsite lies on accessible BLM land.

name: Elko, NV - Central Pacific Division Point
description: Elko, Nevada was a major division point on the Central Pacific Railroad where crews changed, locomotives were serviced, and trains were made up. Established in 1868 as a railroad town, it served as the commercial hub for northeastern Nevada's ranching and mining industries. The original depot area and surrounding historic district retain substantial artifact density from the 1870s-1910s era, including railroad hardware, coins, and commercial tokens. The section house ruins east of town are on BLM land and regularly yield period artifacts.

name: Winnemucca, NV - Central Pacific Water Stop
description: Winnemucca was a critical water stop on the Central Pacific route through the Nevada high desert. The railroad established a substantial service facility here in 1868, including a roundhouse, machine shops, and water tanks fed by the Humboldt River. The combination of a busy railroad facility and the historic Humboldt Trail makes the area around Winnemucca exceptionally productive for metal detecting. Chinese railroad worker campsites, abandoned section houses, and the original depot vicinity yield 1860s-1890s artifacts including coins, personal effects, and railroad hardware.

name: Carlin, NV - Central Pacific Division Point
description: Carlin, Nevada was established in 1868 as a Central Pacific division point in the Humboldt River canyon. The town housed railroad workers and their families in a remote desert setting, with a roundhouse, machine shops, and substantial service facilities. Abandoned section house sites and worker camps in the canyons east and west of Carlin are accessible on BLM land and have produced Civil War-era coins, Chinese artifacts, and railroad hardware dating to the construction period. The canyon walls preserved numerous artifact deposits from the construction crews of 1868.

name: Battle Mountain, NV - Central Pacific Stop
description: Battle Mountain, Nevada was a Central Pacific station established in 1870 at a natural stopping point along the Humboldt River. Named for a reported battle between emigrants and Native Americans in 1857, the town served as a supply hub for surrounding mining districts. Railroad section houses, a depot, and worker camps concentrated artifacts from the 1870s-1900s in this area. Several abandoned mining camps accessible from Battle Mountain via BLM roads have produced material from both the railroad era and earlier emigrant trail period.

name: Humboldt, NV - Central Pacific Station
description: Humboldt Station was an early Central Pacific water and fuel stop along the Humboldt River that was largely abandoned after the railroad established larger facilities at nearby towns. The original station site is now on BLM land and retains excellent artifact density from the construction and early operations period (1868-1885). Chinese worker camps in the surrounding hills have produced coins, tokens, and personal effects. The abandoned grade approaching from the east passes through several undisturbed sections where railroad hardware and construction artifacts are regularly found.

name: Reno, NV - Central Pacific Division Point
description: Reno was established as a railroad town and Central Pacific division point when tracks arrived from the east in 1868. Named after Civil War General Jesse Reno, the town rapidly grew into a regional commercial hub. The original Reno depot area and the older residential districts near the Truckee River have produced substantial quantities of 1870s-1890s coins and personal items. The areas along the original railroad grade approaching Reno from the east through the Truckee Meadows are productive for finding railroad-era artifacts.

name: Wadsworth, NV - Abandoned Railroad Town
description: Wadsworth was the original Central Pacific division point east of Reno and housed a major roundhouse and machine shop complex from 1868. When the division was moved to Sparks in 1904, Wadsworth was largely abandoned, leaving behind intact subsurface deposits from over 35 years of heavy railroad operations. The townsite is on private and BLM land east of present-day Wadsworth and is one of the most productive abandoned railroad town sites in Nevada, regularly yielding 1870s-1900s coins, tokens, railroad hardware, and personal effects from the large worker population.

name: Promontory, UT - Abandoned Town
description: The town of Promontory grew up near the famous Golden Spike site to serve railroad operations on the original line. When the Lucin Cutoff across Great Salt Lake bypassed Promontory Point in 1904, the town quickly emptied, leaving behind an extraordinary concentration of railroad-era artifacts. The entire townsite and surrounding work camps are within Golden Spike National Historic Site or on BLM land, preserving artifacts from both the 1869 construction period and the 1870s-1904 operations era. Railroad spikes, coins, camp equipment, and Chinese worker artifacts are commonly found on the original grade.

name: Kelton, UT - Abandoned Railroad Town
description: Kelton was a Central Pacific station in the remote northwestern Utah desert that served as a freighting hub for Idaho and Montana. During the 1870s-1880s, millions of dollars in goods and silver bullion passed through Kelton's freight depots. The town was completely abandoned by the 1920s when improved roads bypassed the area. The townsite on BLM land is accessible by dirt road and is considered one of Utah's most productive ghost town metal detecting sites, with 1870s-1900s coins, freight-related hardware, and personal effects regularly found.

name: Terrace, UT - Abandoned Railroad Town
description: Terrace was a Union Pacific/Central Pacific service town and water stop in the barren Lucin Desert of northwestern Utah. At its peak in the 1880s it had several hundred residents, a hotel, saloons, and a roundhouse. When the Lucin Cutoff (1904) bypassed the northern Utah route, Terrace was abandoned almost overnight. The BLM-accessible townsite preserves remarkable artifact density from the 1870s-1900s, with coins, personal items, saloon artifacts, railroad hardware, and even occasional gold coins from the town's transient worker population regularly recovered.

name: Piedmont, WY - Abandoned Charcoal & Railroad Town
description: Piedmont, Wyoming was a Union Pacific station and charcoal production center where massive ovens burned local timber for the Union Pacific's locomotives. The town had a significant population of workers operating the kilns and maintaining the railroad in the 1870s-1880s. Three of the original beehive charcoal ovens still stand on BLM land. The surrounding townsite and worker camp areas are highly productive for metal detecting, with coins, railroad spikes, charcoal production equipment, and personal effects from the large multinational workforce that operated here.

name: Bryan, WY - Abandoned Railroad Town
description: Bryan, Wyoming was a Union Pacific division point on the Green River that boomed when the railroad arrived and became a raucous end-of-tracks town in 1868. Named for Major William Bryan, it served as a major supply center for the surrounding region before being largely eclipsed by Green River City. The original townsite along the Green River is accessible on BLM land and has produced an impressive variety of 1868-1880s artifacts including gold and silver coins, gambling tokens, saloon artifacts, and Union Pacific construction hardware.

name: Bear River City, WY - End-of-Track Town
description: Bear River City was one of the notorious Hell on Wheels towns that followed Union Pacific construction crews westward in 1868. These temporary end-of-tracks camps attracted gamblers, whiskey sellers, and outlaws alongside the railroad workforce. Bear River City was the scene of a violent riot in November 1868 that left multiple dead. The townsite is on BLM land and is one of the most sought-after metal detecting locations in Wyoming, with abundant 1868-era coins, gambling paraphernalia, weapons-related artifacts, and personal effects from the transient population.

name: Rawlins, WY - Union Pacific Division Point
description: Rawlins, Wyoming was established as a Union Pacific division point in 1868, named for General John Rawlins of General Grant's staff. It housed a large roundhouse complex and machine shops that were active through the steam era. The town served as a supply depot for the surrounding Carbon Basin ranching and mining country. Areas around the original Union Pacific depot and the old residential blocks near the tracks have produced substantial quantities of 1870s-1910s coins and personal items. The section house ruins east of town on BLM land are particularly productive.

name: Borie, WY - Abandoned Railroad Town
description: Borie was a small Union Pacific station near Cheyenne that served as a helper locomotive base for trains ascending Sherman Hill, the highest point on the transcontinental line. The station and its associated roundhouse were abandoned when modern diesel locomotives made helper service unnecessary. The abandoned facility on accessible BLM-adjacent land has produced railroad hardware and coins from the steam era. The Sherman Hill corridor near Borie contains numerous abandoned maintenance structures and section houses that preserve 1870s-1940s railroad artifacts.

name: Laramie, WY - Union Pacific Division Point
description: Laramie, Wyoming was established as a Union Pacific division point in 1868 and quickly became one of the most important towns in Wyoming Territory. Home to major railroad repair shops, a roundhouse, and the Wyoming Territorial Prison, Laramie processed enormous amounts of traffic on the transcontinental line. The areas around the original Union Pacific shops (now a park) and along the old rail corridors have produced extensive collections of 1870s-1900s coins, railroad hardware, and personal effects. The abandoned section houses and water stops west of Laramie approaching the Continental Divide are excellent detecting sites on BLM land.

name: Cheyenne, WY - Union Pacific Division HQ
description: Cheyenne was born as the Union Pacific's construction headquarters in 1867 and instantly became the largest city in Wyoming Territory. The Union Pacific's massive repair shops and roundhouses made it one of the most important railroad centers in the West. As an early Hell on Wheels town, Cheyenne hosted thousands of transient workers, gamblers, and merchants. The historic blocks around the original UP depot and along the old stockyards area regularly produce 1870s-1890s coins and artifacts. The section house sites east of Cheyenne near Sidney, NE are productive BLM land detecting areas.

name: North Platte, NE - Union Pacific Division Point
description: North Platte was the location of Union Pacific's famous 'end-of-track' camp in December 1866, where 10,000 workers spent the winter. A temporary city of tents and quickly built wooden structures, it was one of the original Hell on Wheels towns. North Platte later became a major Union Pacific division point with extensive yards and shop facilities. The original camp areas along the Platte River bottom are on accessible land and have produced gold coins, gambling tokens, military artifacts from Civil War veterans in the railroad workforce, and construction-era hardware.

name: Dodge, KS - Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe Division Point
description: Dodge City, Kansas was one of the most famous railroad towns in the West, established when the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway arrived in 1872. As the end of cattle drives from Texas, Dodge City saw enormous volumes of money change hands in its saloons, gambling halls, and stock pens. Lawmen like Wyatt Earp and Bat Masterson patrolled the famous Front Street. The historic downtown area, Boot Hill Cemetery vicinity, and old cattle pens areas around the Arkansas River have produced impressive quantities of 1870s-1890s coins, gaming tokens, and personal items from this legendary cow town.

name: La Junta, CO - AT&SF Division Point
description: La Junta, Colorado was a major Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe division point at the junction of the main line and the Pueblo branch. It housed extensive railroad facilities including a roundhouse, machine shops, and the Fred Harvey House restaurant hotel. The town's position at the junction of the Mountain and Cimarron branches of the old Santa Fe Trail made it a crossroads of historical activity from the 1870s onward. The old AT&SF shop areas and the nearby Bent's Old Fort National Historic Site make La Junta one of the richest combined railroad/frontier detecting areas in Colorado.

name: Raton Pass, NM/CO - AT&SF Mountain Crossing
description: Raton Pass was the critical mountain crossing point where the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway entered New Mexico from Colorado in 1878, after a famous race with the Denver and Rio Grande for control of the strategic pass. The 7,834-foot summit was the scene of enormous construction activity and heavy train operations for decades. Section houses, helper locomotive bases, and maintenance facilities dotted the pass. The abandoned grade sections along the original alignment and the ruins of Raton Pass summit facilities on USFS land are excellent detecting sites with 1880s-1920s railroad artifacts.

name: Lamy, NM - AT&SF Junction
description: Lamy, New Mexico was the AT&SF junction point where the mainline to the west met the branch to Santa Fe. The original Santa Fe depot (now a hotel) and surrounding structures make Lamy a well-preserved railroad community. As a crew change point and junction, the station saw heavy traffic from the 1880s through the mid-20th century. The surrounding desert landscape preserves artifact deposits from the railroad operations period and occasional Spanish Colonial-era items related to the El Camino Real corridor. BLM land adjacent to the town is productive for early railroad-era detecting.

name: Winslow, AZ - AT&SF Division Point
description: Winslow, Arizona was one of the most important Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe division points in the desert Southwest. It hosted a major roundhouse, repair shops, and a famous Fred Harvey hotel (La Posada, still operating). The surrounding Navajo and Hopi lands saw extensive commercial activity related to the railroad from the 1880s onward. The historic original depot area and the ruins of the old AT&SF facilities at the edge of town have produced coins, railroad hardware, and Fred Harvey-era artifacts. The abandoned section house sites east of Winslow on BLM land are productive detecting locations.

name: Needles, CA - AT&SF Division Point
description: Needles, California was a critical Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe division point at the Colorado River crossing, where crews changed and locomotives were serviced before the brutal Mojave Desert crossing. The town's position at the gateway to the Mojave made it a vital watering point for both trains and passengers. The original AT&SF depot area and the abandoned section house ruins along the Colorado River have produced 1890s-1930s coins, railroad hardware, and personal effects from the thousands of workers stationed here. The BLM desert land east of Needles preserves intact railroad-era sites.

name: El Paso, TX/NM - Southern Pacific Junction
description: El Paso was the major junction where the Southern Pacific Sunset Route met the Texas and Pacific Railway and the AT&SF line into New Mexico. As a border city with significant railroad operations, El Paso was a hub of commerce and transient population from the 1880s through the early 20th century. The old Union Depot area, the original Chihuahuita neighborhood near the Rio Grande, and the abandoned yards east of downtown have produced substantial quantities of 1880s-1920s coins and personal items. The section house ruins west of El Paso in the desert on BLM land are excellent detecting sites.

name: Lordsburg, NM - Southern Pacific Division Point
description: Lordsburg, New Mexico was established as a Southern Pacific division point when the tracks arrived in 1880. Situated in the high Chihuahuan Desert, it served the surrounding mining districts of the Boot Heel country. The old SP depot and the surrounding original townsite have produced coins and personal items from the 1880s-1920s. The abandoned section houses and water stops between Lordsburg and Deming on BLM land are particularly accessible and productive for railroad-era detecting, with the dry desert climate preserving metal artifacts exceptionally well.

name: Tucson, AZ - Southern Pacific Division Point
description: Tucson, Arizona was established as a major Southern Pacific division point when the Sunset Route arrived in 1880. The arrival of the railroad transformed the old Spanish and Mexican colonial town into a boom city almost overnight. The area around the original 1880s SP depot (Congress Street) and the old Barrio Historico neighborhood have produced impressive quantities of Spanish-Mexican colonial items, Civil War-era artifacts from the Army post, and railroad-era coins and hardware. The abandoned section facilities east of Tucson along the original grade are on BLM-adjacent land.

name: Yuma, AZ - Southern Pacific River Crossing
description: Yuma, Arizona was the site of the critical Colorado River crossing for the Southern Pacific, completed in 1877. The Army's Fort Yuma on the California side and the SP facilities on the Arizona side made this a concentration of military and railroad activity. The approach to the original railroad bridge and the fort ruins on the bluffs have produced Spanish colonial, Mexican, military, and railroad-era artifacts spanning nearly 200 years of occupation. The old Yuma Territorial Prison site and the surrounding BLM desert land preserve exceptional artifact density.

name: Indio, CA - Southern Pacific Station
description: Indio, California was a Southern Pacific water and fuel stop in the Coachella Valley, established in 1876. Its position at the edge of the Salton Sink, below sea level, made water supply critical for locomotive operations. The original SP depot area and the surrounding desert have produced 1880s-1920s railroad artifacts. The BLM desert land east of Indio along the original Sunset Route grade contains numerous intact section house ruins and water tank sites that regularly yield railroad-era coins and hardware in excellent condition due to the dry desert climate.

name: Houston, TX - Southern Pacific Junction
description: Houston was a major Southern Pacific junction and maintenance hub where several SP lines converged. The old SP yards and the surrounding Harrisburg area, the original terminus of the first Texas railroad (1840s), make Houston one of the richest multi-period railroad detecting areas in the South. The Harrisburg townsite (now part of Houston's east side) and the early SP shop areas have produced coins and artifacts spanning the 1840s through the early 20th century. The original SP roundhouse areas in the East End neighborhood preserve good artifact density.

name: Bismarck, ND - Northern Pacific Division Point
description: Bismarck, North Dakota was the temporary western terminus of the Northern Pacific Railroad from 1872 to 1879, during which time it was the jumping-off point for expeditions into the Black Hills and the center of Sioux War-era military activity. General Custer departed from nearby Fort Abraham Lincoln for his last campaign. The original NP depot area and the Fort Abraham Lincoln vicinity have produced extraordinary quantities of military and railroad artifacts from the 1870s. The fort site is a state historic site, but the surrounding bottomland areas on BLM-accessible land are productive.

name: Miles City, MT - Northern Pacific Division Point
description: Miles City, Montana was the major Northern Pacific division point in eastern Montana, established near the confluence of the Tongue and Yellowstone Rivers in 1881. It was the center of the Montana cattle industry during the open range era and home to Fort Keogh, a major Army post. The combination of railroad, military, and cattle industry activity from the 1870s-1900s makes Miles City one of the most historically productive detecting areas in Montana. The old NP yards, the Fort Keogh vicinity, and the nearby Tongue River bottomlands on BLM land regularly yield military, railroad, and range-era artifacts.

name: Livingston, MT - Northern Pacific Division Point
description: Livingston, Montana was the Northern Pacific's gateway to Yellowstone National Park and a major division point with extensive repair facilities. Established in 1882, it quickly became one of the most important railroad towns in Montana. The massive NP repair shops (still standing as a museum) processed thousands of locomotives through the steam era. The original NP depot area and the streets around the historic railroad district have produced excellent quantities of 1880s-1920s coins and railroad artifacts. The section house ruins in Paradise Valley south of Livingston on BLM/USFS land are particularly productive.

name: Missoula, MT - Northern Pacific Division Point
description: Missoula was a Northern Pacific division point established when the railroad arrived in 1883, situated where the Clark Fork River meets several mountain valleys. It housed a substantial NP roundhouse and shops. Fort Missoula, established to protect railroad construction workers and the Clark Fork valley, adds significant military artifact potential to the area. The original NP depot vicinity and the Fort Missoula grounds have produced 1880s-1900s military and railroad artifacts. The section house ruins along the Clark Fork River west of Missoula on BLM land are productive for detecting.

name: Spokane, WA - Northern Pacific Junction
description: Spokane, Washington was a major Northern Pacific junction where several lines met to serve the Inland Empire and the mining districts of Idaho and Montana. Established when the NP arrived in 1881, it grew rapidly to become the largest city between Minneapolis and Seattle. The area around the original NP depot in downtown Spokane and the historic Peaceful Valley neighborhood near the Spokane Falls have produced 1880s-1910s coins and personal items. The abandoned NP section facilities east of Spokane on the Idaho border are accessible on BLM land.

name: Silverton, CO - Denver & Rio Grande Terminus
description: Silverton, Colorado was the terminus of the famous Durango & Silverton narrow gauge line, deep in the San Juan Mountains at 9,318 feet elevation. When the railroad arrived in 1882, it connected the silver and gold mines of the San Juan region to markets. The town saw explosive growth during the mining boom and served as a shipping point for ore from dozens of surrounding mines. The original D&RG depot area and the abandoned mine sites accessible from Silverton on BLM/USFS land are exceptional metal detecting locations, with 1880s-1920s coins, mining equipment, and personal effects from the multinational mining workforce.

name: Durango, CO - Denver & Rio Grande Division Point
description: Durango, Colorado was established specifically as a Denver & Rio Grande division point and smelting center in 1880, positioned to process ore from the surrounding San Juan mining district. The town was built from scratch by the railroad and grew rapidly to house smelter workers, railroad employees, and the supply trade for surrounding mining camps. The historic downtown near the original D&RG depot and the smelter site on the Animas River have produced excellent quantities of 1880s-1920s coins and artifacts. The D&RG grade north toward Silverton preserves numerous section house ruins on USFS land.

name: Canon City, CO - Denver & Rio Grande Junction
description: Canon City was the point from which the Denver & Rio Grande entered the famous Royal Gorge of the Arkansas River after winning the 'Royal Gorge War' against the AT&SF in 1879. The town had substantial D&RG facilities including a roundhouse and repair shops. The Royal Gorge approach and the original depot area have produced 1880s-1920s railroad and mining artifacts. The D&RG grade through the Royal Gorge (partly accessible via the Royal Gorge Route Railroad or on foot) preserves section house ruins and construction camp sites from the 1879-1880 construction period.

name: Salida, CO - Denver & Rio Grande Division Point
description: Salida, Colorado was one of the most important Denver & Rio Grande division points in the Arkansas River valley, serving as a hub for lines branching to Gunnison, the San Luis Valley, and over Marshall Pass. The large D&RG roundhouse and repair shops employed hundreds of workers. The historic downtown blocks near the original depot and the old shop district along the Arkansas River have produced substantial quantities of 1880s-1920s coins and railroad artifacts. The D&RG grade west of Salida over Marshall Pass (now abandoned) preserves intact section house ruins accessible on BLM/USFS land.

name: Gunnison, CO - Denver & Rio Grande Station
description: Gunnison, Colorado was reached by the Denver & Rio Grande in 1881 after the narrow gauge line crossed Marshall Pass, opening the coal fields of the Gunnison country to market. The town served as a supply hub for numerous mining camps in the surrounding mountains. The original D&RG depot vicinity and the historic blocks near the railroad have produced coins and artifacts from the 1881-1920s period. The abandoned narrow gauge grade west of Gunnison through the Black Canyon approach and north toward Crested Butte on BLM/USFS land preserves intact section house sites.

name: Antonito, CO - Denver & Rio Grande Junction
description: Antonito was the junction where the Cumbres & Toltec Scenic Railroad (formerly D&RG) narrow gauge line branches from the San Luis Valley main line to cross Cumbres Pass into New Mexico. The original junction facilities and the surrounding Hispanic farming community have produced a mix of late 19th century railroad artifacts and earlier Spanish Colonial-era items. The Cumbres & Toltec grade through Toltec Gorge on the Colorado/New Mexico border (accessible by the scenic railroad) preserves excellent section house ruins and construction camp sites from the 1880 construction push.

name: Newton, KS - AT&SF Division Point
description: Newton, Kansas was the original terminus of the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway (1871) and briefly the wildest cattle shipping town in Kansas before Wichita and later Dodge City. The Newton 'Massacre' in 1871 left six dead in a famous saloon gunfight. Newton then settled into a respectable division point role with extensive railroad shop facilities. The original AT&SF shop areas and the historic downtown blocks near the original depot have produced 1870s-1890s coins, cattle drive artifacts, and railroad hardware. The KBS (Kansas Biological Survey) lands south of Newton near the old cattle pens are productive.

name: Abilene, KS - Kansas Pacific Station
description: Abilene, Kansas was the first major Kansas Pacific cattle shipping terminus, where the famous Chisholm Trail delivered Texas longhorns for shipment east from 1867 to 1871. The town was marshal'd by Wild Bill Hickok in 1871. The original stockyards area south of the tracks, the site of the old McCoy House hotel, and the historic downtown blocks have produced impressive quantities of 1867-1880s gold and silver coins, cattle-era tokens, and personal items from cowboys, gamblers, and railroad workers. The Smoky Hill Trail/Kansas Pacific corridor east of Abilene on accessible farmland is highly productive.

name: Ellsworth, KS - Kansas Pacific Cattle Town
description: Ellsworth, Kansas was a major Kansas Pacific cattle shipping town and division point in the early 1870s. It briefly supplanted Abilene as the primary cattle terminus and saw intense commerce and violence during the cattle drive era. The original townsite near the Smoky Hill River and the Kansas Pacific grade have produced 1860s-1880s gold coins, cattle brand tokens, and personal effects from the colorful transient population. The BLM accessible land along the Smoky Hill River west of Ellsworth retains good artifact density from the cattle era and the earlier Smoky Hill Trail period.

name: Hays, KS - Kansas Pacific Division Point
description: Hays, Kansas was established as a Kansas Pacific division point and was one of the most violent frontier towns of the 1860s-1870s. Fort Hays, established nearby to protect railroad construction workers, was a major Army post. Wild Bill Hickok served as city marshal in 1869. The area around Fort Hays State Historic Site and the original downtown blocks near the Kansas Pacific depot have produced substantial quantities of 1860s-1880s military and railroad artifacts. The fort grounds and the Smoky Hill Trail corridor accessible from Hays are among the most productive metal detecting areas in western Kansas.

name: Ogden, UT - Union Pacific/Central Pacific Junction
description: Ogden became the official junction point of the Union Pacific and Central Pacific railroads after the Golden Spike ceremony, handling all transcontinental traffic from 1869 onward. The Ogden Union Station became one of the most important railroad facilities in the West. Ogden's 25th Street, once known for its saloons and vice establishments serving railroad workers, is one of the most historically artifact-rich streets in Utah. The original rail yards and historic Junction neighborhood have produced extensive 1870s-1910s coins and railroad artifacts. The Chinese worker camps near the old yards are documented artifact sites.

name: Echo, UT - Union Pacific Construction Camp
description: Echo Canyon, Utah was the route by which the Union Pacific descended from the Wyoming high plains into the Utah basin in 1868-1869. The canyon was heavily fortified by Brigham Young's forces who feared a repeat of Missouri-style expulsion, but the confrontation was resolved peacefully. Extensive construction camps were established in and near Echo Canyon during the winter of 1868-1869. The abandoned camps and section house ruins in Echo Canyon (accessible via I-80 corridor and BLM land) regularly yield 1868-1870s coins, construction hardware, and personal effects from the large Irish and Scandinavian workforce.

name: Evanston, WY - Union Pacific Division Point
description: Evanston, Wyoming was a major Union Pacific division point near the Utah border, established in 1869. It housed a substantial roundhouse and repair facility that served transcontinental trains for decades. A significant Chinese community developed in Evanston to serve the railroad and the local coal mines. The historic Chinatown area and the original Union Pacific shop grounds have produced coins, Chinese artifacts, and railroad hardware from the 1870s-1900s era. The Piedmont charcoal ovens northeast of Evanston on BLM land are easily accessible and extremely productive for metal detecting.

name: Green River, WY - Union Pacific Division Point
description: Green River City, Wyoming was established when the Union Pacific reached the Green River in 1868, quickly becoming one of the most important division points on the transcontinental line. The railroad facilities here included an extensive roundhouse and the first railroad bridge over the Green River. The town was also an outfitting point for John Wesley Powell's famous 1869 Colorado River expedition. The original townsite along the river and the abandoned section houses east and west of Green River on BLM land are productive detecting areas, with 1868-1900s coins, expedition-era artifacts, and railroad hardware.

name: Bingham Canyon, UT - Utah Central Railroad
description: The Utah Central Railroad (later Utah Southern) served the vast mining districts of the Oquirrh Mountains, including the Bingham Canyon copper mines that would become the world's largest open-pit mine. Branch lines up Bingham Canyon served dozens of mines from the 1870s-1920s. The abandoned mine communities and railroad grades above the modern open pit are rich metal detecting sites on BLM land. Pre-mine era homestead and miner camps from the 1870s-1910s regularly yield coins, mining equipment, and personal effects. The original Utah Central grade toward Provo has produced early railroad-era artifacts.

name: Winfield, KS - AT&SF Branch Point
description: Winfield, Kansas was an important Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe branch point in southern Kansas and served as the county seat of Cowley County during the cattle drive era. Several thousand homesteaders and cattlemen used Winfield as a supply point from the 1870s onward. The original town square area, the early AT&SF depot vicinity, and the fairgrounds areas near the old cattle pens have produced 1875-1910s coins and personal items. The Kansas-Oklahoma border region south of Winfield near the historic Chisholm Trail corridor on accessible land is highly productive.

name: Mojave, CA - AT&SF/SP Junction
description: Mojave, California was a critical junction where the Southern Pacific (Sunset Route) met the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe branch coming down from Barstow. Established in 1876, it served as a water and fuel stop in the northern Mojave Desert. The surrounding desert landscape has preserved artifact deposits from 1876 through the 1930s in excellent condition due to the dry climate. The old SP depot area and the abandoned section house ruins along the highway 58 corridor on BLM land regularly yield railroad-era coins, hardware, and personal effects.

name: Caliente, NV - SP Tunnel District
description: Caliente, Nevada was a Southern Pacific division point in the Meadow Valley Wash where the SP constructed a series of tunnels through the challenging terrain of eastern Nevada. The railroad facilities here employed hundreds of workers maintaining the tunnels and servicing trains. The tunnels and their adjacent construction camps (1900s era) are on BLM land and regularly yield coins and artifacts from the large tunnel construction workforce. The original SP depot (still standing as city hall) and the surrounding historic blocks have produced 1905-1940s coins and railroad artifacts.

name: Milford, UT - Utah & Nevada Railroad Terminal
description: Milford, Utah was the terminal of the Utah & Nevada Railroad (later Union Pacific branch) that served the Frisco and other western Utah mining districts. The town's smelter processed ore from surrounding mines from the 1870s-1920s. The original smelter site, the U&N terminal facilities, and the townsite along the railroad have produced 1875-1920s mining and railroad artifacts. The abandoned Frisco mining camp north of Milford on BLM land is one of Utah's premier ghost town metal detecting sites, with intact subsurface deposits from the silver mining boom.

name: Eureka, NV - Nevada Central Railroad
description: Eureka, Nevada was served by the Nevada Central narrow gauge railroad (1875) and was one of the richest silver-lead mining towns in Nevada's history. The smelters consumed enormous quantities of charcoal made by Italian charcoal burners in the surrounding mountains, whose stone kiln ruins are scattered on BLM land. The historic downtown, the smelter ruins along the valley, and the Nevada Central grade have produced exceptional quantities of 1875-1910s coins including Seated Liberty and Barber-era silver. The charcoal kiln sites in the Diamond Mountains accessible from Eureka are extremely productive for metal detecting.

name: Virginia City, NV - Virginia & Truckee Railroad
description: Virginia City was served by the famous Virginia & Truckee Railroad (1869), one of the wealthiest short lines in American history that hauled Comstock Lode silver ore to mills along the Carson River. The V&T was eventually abandoned in 1950, but its grade from Virginia City to Carson City passes through the heart of Comstock country. The Virginia City townsite, the V&T grade, and the surrounding Comstock Lode mining claims on BLM/USFS land are among the most productive metal detecting areas in the United States, regularly yielding Seated Liberty silver, early gold coins, and mining-era personal effects.

name: Tonopah, NV - Tonopah & Goldfield Railroad
description: Tonopah and nearby Goldfield were served by the Tonopah & Goldfield Railroad (1904), connecting the last great Nevada silver boom camps to the main SP line. These boomtowns generated more wealth in the 1900s-1920s than almost any other Nevada mining district. The original T&G grade and the surrounding Tonopah townsite have produced Liberty Head and Indian Head gold coins, silver trade items, and mining camp artifacts. The abandoned sections of the T&G grade on BLM land between Tonopah and Goldfield are accessible and preserve intact camp sites from the 1900s-1920s mining era.

name: Goldfield, NV - Tonopah & Goldfield Railroad Terminus
description: Goldfield, Nevada was briefly the largest city in Nevada (1907-1910) during the gold rush that produced over $86 million in gold. The Tonopah & Goldfield Railroad depot and the surrounding streets of what was once a city of 30,000 are now largely ghost town and BLM land. The abandoned residential blocks, the original depot area, and the surrounding mine sites are among the most productive early 20th century metal detecting locations in the West. Liberty Head gold coins, Barber-era silver, and mining camp artifacts are regularly found in the surviving building foundations and around the original business district.

name: Fallon, NV - SP Branch Line
description: Fallon, Nevada was served by a Southern Pacific branch line and was the center of the Churchill Valley irrigation district from the early 1900s. The surrounding desert preserves excellent artifact deposits from the homesteading era (1903-1920s) and earlier emigrant trail period along the Carson Sink. The area east of Fallon near the old Lovelock Cave and the sink itself on BLM land contains artifacts from multiple eras including Native American, emigrant trail, and railroad settlement periods. The abandoned homestead sites near the SP branch grade are productive for early 20th century artifacts.

name: Boron, CA - AT&SF Borax Line
description: Boron, California was a major AT&SF station on the Mojave Desert that served the massive borax mining operations of Kern County. The historic borax mining camps, the AT&SF depot area, and the surrounding Mojave Desert on BLM land preserve excellent artifact density from the early 1900s borax mining era and the earlier Death Valley borax operations. The Twenty Mule Team Road, over which borax was hauled before the railroad arrived, passes through BLM land and preserves wagon hardware, personal effects, and early mining artifacts. The original mining camp sites near Boron are highly productive for early 20th century detecting.

name: Lordsburg, NM - Southern Pacific Water Stop
description: Lordsburg, New Mexico was a critical Southern Pacific water stop in the Animas Valley where the railroad maintained a depot, section houses, and water facilities for the hot desert crossing. The area around the original SP facilities and the old townsite have produced coins and personal items from the 1880s-1930s. The surrounding Hidalgo County desert on BLM land preserves numerous section house sites along the original SP grade that are accessible for metal detecting. The ghost town of Shakespeare, just 2 miles from Lordsburg, adds further historical context with 1870s-1880s mining artifacts.

name: Deming, NM - SP/AT&SF Junction
description: Deming, New Mexico was the junction where the Southern Pacific's Sunset Route met the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe's Benson line in 1881, completing the second transcontinental railroad. The famous 'silver spike' ceremony at Deming echoed the Golden Spike event at Promontory. As a major railroad junction in the Mimbres Valley, Deming saw constant traffic from two major railroads. The original depot area, the old Deming townsite, and the surrounding accessible BLM desert land have produced substantial quantities of 1880s-1910s junction town artifacts including coins, railroad hardware, and personal effects.

name: Willcox, AZ - SP Division Point
description: Willcox, Arizona was a Southern Pacific division point in the Sulphur Springs Valley that served as the cattle shipping center for southeastern Arizona. Established in 1880, it quickly became the shipping point for cattle raised on the vast ranches of Cochise County, including those of the famous Chiricahua Apache country. The original SP depot area and the old stockyards vicinity have produced 1880s-1920s cattle era and railroad artifacts. The surrounding BLM land in the Sulphur Springs Valley and the approaches to the Dragoon Mountains preserve excellent artifact deposits from the Apache-era period.

name: Maricopa, AZ - SP Water Stop
description: Maricopa Wells was a critical water stop on the Southern Pacific line through the Sonoran Desert, established at the historic Maricopa Wells stagecoach station that had served the Butterfield Overland Mail. The combination of the earlier stagecoach era (1857-1861) and the railroad era (1879 onward) makes this site one of the most multi-layered detecting locations in Arizona. The BLM land surrounding the original Maricopa Wells site preserves artifacts from both periods. Stagecoach-era items, railroad hardware, and desert crossroad artifacts from multiple eras are regularly found at this site.

name: Rincon, NM - SP Water & Fuel Stop
description: Rincon, New Mexico was a Southern Pacific water and coal fueling stop at the junction with the Rio Grande valley where the main SP line turned north toward Albuquerque. Established in 1880, it maintained continuous operations as a section point and water stop. The original Rincon townsite on accessible land and the SP facilities ruins along the Rio Grande have produced 1880s-1920s artifacts. The surrounding Rio Grande bosque and desert BLM land preserve good artifact density from both the railroad era and the earlier Camino Real period.

name: Marysvale, UT - D&RG Branch Terminal
description: Marysvale, Utah was the terminus of a Denver & Rio Grande branch line serving the gold mines of the Piute County mountains. The narrow gauge branch hauled ore from the Alunite mines and connected this isolated mountain valley to markets. The original D&RG terminal facilities, the historic townsite, and the surrounding mine sites on BLM land have produced 1880s-1930s mining and railroad artifacts. The abandoned mine camps accessible from Marysvale on BLM roads in the Tushar Mountains are particularly productive for coins and personal effects from the gold mining era.

name: Helper, UT - D&RG Helper District
description: Helper, Utah was the Denver & Rio Grande base of operations for helper locomotives that pushed heavy coal trains up the steep grade of Price Canyon. The town was a major railroad labor community housing hundreds of workers and their families. The surrounding Carbon County coalfields were served by numerous D&RG branches into the coal camps. The original D&RG facilities and the surrounding ghost town coal camps on BLM land north of Helper (Winter Quarters, Castle Gate) are excellent detecting sites with 1880s-1920s railroad and mining artifacts.

name: Price, UT - D&RG Coal Country Hub
description: Price, Utah was the D&RG division point for the coalfields of Carbon County, with branch lines reaching dozens of coal camps in the surrounding Book Cliffs and Wasatch Plateau. The surrounding area includes numerous completely abandoned coal mining towns (Castle Gate, Sunnyside, Standardville) on BLM land that preserve extraordinary artifact density from the 1890s-1940s. Railroad and mining artifacts, company store tokens, and personal effects from the diverse immigrant workforce (Italian, Greek, Japanese, Slavic workers) are regularly found at these sites.

name: Moffat Tunnel, CO - D&RG/D&SL Entrance
description: The Moffat Tunnel (1928) was one of the greatest railroad engineering achievements in the Rockies, cutting 6.2 miles through the Continental Divide under James Peak to eliminate the grueling Rollins Pass crossing. The construction camps at both the east portal (Winter Park area) and west portal (Rollinsville) employed thousands of workers for several years. The USFS land surrounding both portals and the abandoned construction worker camps preserve 1920s-era coins, camp equipment, and personal effects. The original Rollins Pass route (abandoned after the tunnel opened) has numerous section house ruins accessible on USFS land.

name: Buford, WY - Abandoned Sherman Summit Town
description: Buford, Wyoming sits near the summit of Sherman Hill on the original transcontinental railroad grade, the highest point on the Union Pacific at 8,247 feet. The original Sherman station and surrounding worker community were significant during the steam era when helper locomotives were required for the steep grade. The abandoned original station site and the Sherman Monument vicinity on accessible land preserve artifacts from both the 1869 construction period and the long operational era through the steam locomotive age. The original grade near the summit regularly yields railroad spikes, coins, and maintenance hardware.

name: Cisco, UT - D&RG Remote Desert Station
description: Cisco, Utah was a remote Denver & Rio Grande station and cattle loading point on the Colorado Plateau where the D&RG line crossed the Book Cliffs. Largely abandoned by the 1980s, the original Cisco townsite on BLM land is a well-known Colorado Plateau ghost town. The abandonment preserved the late 19th/early 20th century character of the townsite. The surrounding desert on BLM land retains artifact deposits from the 1883-1950s operational period. Railroad hardware, cattle drive-era artifacts, and coins from the isolated station community are regularly found here.

name: Thompson Springs, UT - D&RG Station
description: Thompson Springs (now Thompson) was a D&RG station and jumping-off point for early access into Arches and Canyonlands country. The remote high desert location and the BLM land surrounding the nearly abandoned townsite preserve exceptional artifact density. As one of the most isolated stations on the D&RG line through the Colorado Plateau, Thompson Springs accumulated artifacts from ranchers, miners, prospectors, and railroad workers from the 1880s-1950s. The surrounding BLM Colorado Plateau is highly accessible and preserves both the isolated character and the artifact deposits of this remote railroad community.
